meta.title: DBX-MLP-2: Model Deployment, Serving and A/B Testing

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to log a trained scikit-learn model as an artifact in an active MLflow run so it can later be loaded for inference. Which function call correctly logs the model?

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact(model, 'sklearn-model')
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, artifact_path='sklearn-model')
mlflow.log_param('model', model)
mlflow.save_model(model, 'sklearn-model')

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.sklearn.log_model() serializes a scikit-learn estimator and logs it to the active run with full MLflow model metadata (MLmodel file, conda environment, model signature if provided), enabling later loading via mlflow.pyfunc.load_model(). A (log_artifact) logs arbitrary files but does not produce a proper MLflow model directory with MLmodel metadata. C (log_param) stores key-value strings, not model objects. D (save_model) saves to a local path but does not log to the active run's artifact store.
questions.reference: MLflow sklearn flavor — mlflow.sklearn.log_model()

questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between MLflow experiments and runs?

options.text: A run contains multiple experiments, each with different hyperparameters
An experiment is a named container that groups multiple runs, where each run records parameters, metrics, and artifacts
Experiments and runs are the same concept with different names in different MLflow versions
Each experiment can only contain one active run at a time

questions.explanation: B is correct because in MLflow's hierarchy, an experiment is the top-level grouping entity (like a project or model family), and each experiment contains many runs. Each run is one execution of training code that logs its own parameters, metrics, and artifacts. A has the hierarchy inverted. C is wrong — they are distinct concepts at different levels of the hierarchy. D is wrong — an experiment can have many concurrent active runs (useful for parallel hyperparameter searches).
questions.reference: MLflow Tracking — experiment and run hierarchy

questions.stem: A data scientist enables mlflow.autolog() before training an XGBoost model. After training completes, they want to additionally log a custom validation metric computed on a held-out test set. What is the correct approach?

options.text: Call mlflow.autolog(disable=True) and then mlflow.log_metric() to replace all autologged metrics
Within the same active run context (or by passing the run_id), call mlflow.log_metric('test_custom_metric', value) after training
Create a new separate run to log the custom metric so autologged and manual metrics are not mixed
Custom metrics cannot be added to a run that used autologging

questions.explanation: B is correct because autologging and manual logging are not mutually exclusive. Within the same run context opened by autolog (or by explicitly referencing the run_id using mlflow.start_run(run_id=...)), you can call mlflow.log_metric() to add custom metrics alongside autologged ones. A would disable all future autologging but does not correctly allow adding to an existing run. C creates unnecessary run fragmentation and loses the connection between autologged and custom metrics for the same training run. D is false — manual logging is always available regardless of autolog.
questions.reference: MLflow — combining autolog() with manual mlflow.log_metric()

questions.stem: An ML engineer constructs a HyperOpt search space that includes both a continuous learning rate and a categorical choice of optimizer. Which code correctly defines this combined search space?

options.text: space = {'lr': hp.uniform('lr', 1e-4, 1e-1), 'optimizer': hp.choice('optimizer', ['adam', 'sgd', 'rmsprop'])}
space = {'lr': hp.randint('lr', 100), 'optimizer': hp.uniform('optimizer', 0, 2)}
space = {'lr': hp.choice('lr', [1e-4, 1e-1]), 'optimizer': hp.uniform('optimizer', ['adam', 'sgd'])}
space = [hp.uniform(1e-4, 1e-1), hp.choice(['adam', 'sgd', 'rmsprop'])]

questions.explanation: A is correct because hp.uniform() is the appropriate distribution for a continuous float like learning rate, and hp.choice() is correct for a discrete categorical variable like optimizer name. The dict structure with named keys is the standard HyperOpt search space format. B uses hp.randint for a float and hp.uniform for a categorical — both wrong. C misuses hp.choice for learning rate (limiting to exactly two values, not continuous) and misuses hp.uniform for optimizer (which expects numeric bounds, not strings). D uses a list format without named keys, which HyperOpt cannot unpack correctly.
questions.reference: HyperOpt — mixed search spaces with hp.uniform() and hp.choice()

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.models.infer_signature() to create a model signature. Which inputs are required by infer_signature()?

options.text: A list of feature names and their data types as strings
A sample of the model input data and a sample of the model output (predictions)
The trained model object and the training DataFrame
The MLflow run ID and the artifact path of the logged model

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.models.infer_signature(model_input, model_output) takes a representative sample of the input data (e.g., X_train or X_test as a pandas DataFrame or numpy array) and a sample of the model's output predictions to infer both input and output schemas. A is wrong because infer_signature() derives schemas from actual data, not manually specified type strings. C is wrong because infer_signature() does not take the model object — it takes data samples. D is wrong because infer_signature() operates on data, not MLflow metadata.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.models.infer_signature(model_input, model_output)

questions.stem: An ML team runs parallel hyperparameter trials using SparkTrials with HyperOpt. Select TWO behaviors that are true of SparkTrials compared to the standard Trials object.

options.text: SparkTrials distributes each trial as a separate Spark task, allowing multiple trials to run concurrently on cluster workers
SparkTrials automatically logs each trial as a child MLflow run nested under a parent run
SparkTrials enables GPU allocation per trial using the parallelism parameter
SparkTrials requires the objective function to return a dict with 'loss' and 'status' keys, whereas Trials only requires a scalar loss

questions.explanation: A is correct because SparkTrials dispatches each HyperOpt trial as a Spark task to worker nodes, enabling true parallel hyperparameter search across the cluster, unlike the sequential Trials object. B is correct because SparkTrials has built-in MLflow integration that automatically creates a parent run and logs each trial as a nested child run with its parameters and metrics. C is wrong because the parallelism parameter controls the number of concurrent Spark tasks, not GPU allocation — GPU assignment requires cluster configuration, not the parallelism parameter. D is wrong because both Trials and SparkTrials require the same objective function return format (dict with 'loss' and 'status').
questions.reference: HyperOpt SparkTrials — distributed execution and MLflow integration

questions.stem:
A data scientist runs the following code but finds the feature importance figure is NOT visible in the MLflow UI artifacts tab. What is the issue?

```python
with mlflow.start_run():
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    plot_importance(model, ax=ax)
    mlflow.log_artifact('/tmp/importance.png')
```

options.text: plot_importance() does not work with MLflow
The figure was never saved to '/tmp/importance.png' before calling log_artifact()
mlflow.log_artifact() only works with text files, not PNG images
The artifact must be logged before model.fit() completes

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.log_artifact() takes a file path and uploads the file at that path to the artifact store. However, the code never calls fig.savefig('/tmp/importance.png') before calling log_artifact, so the file at that path either does not exist or is from a previous run. The fix is to add fig.savefig('/tmp/importance.png') before the log_artifact call, or use mlflow.log_figure(fig, 'importance.png') to avoid the intermediate file entirely. A is wrong — plot_importance works with matplotlib figures. C is wrong — log_artifact supports any file type including images. D is wrong — artifacts can be logged after training completes.
questions.reference: MLflow — log_artifact() requires file to exist; use log_figure() for in-memory figures

questions.stem: A data scientist compares two MLflow runs and notices that Run A was logged with mlflow.xgboost.autolog() while Run B was logged manually. Which metric will appear in Run A's logged metrics that would NOT typically appear in Run B unless explicitly added?

options.text: validation_0-rmse logged at each boosting round (step-wise metric)
Total training wall-clock time
Python interpreter version
Memory usage per tree

questions.explanation: A is correct because mlflow.xgboost.autolog() logs evaluation metrics (like validation_0-rmse, validation_0-logloss) at every boosting round as a step-wise time series, which would require manual calls to mlflow.log_metric('rmse', value, step=i) in a custom callback to replicate. B (training time) can be logged manually but is not a standard XGBoost autolog output. C (Python version) is captured in the conda environment file, not as a metric. D (memory per tree) is not logged by XGBoost autolog.
questions.reference: MLflow XGBoost autolog — step-wise metric logging per boosting round

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to store the path to the training dataset used in an experiment as metadata on the run for reproducibility. Which MLflow function is most appropriate?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')
mlflow.set_tag('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')
mlflow.log_artifact('/mnt/data/train.parquet')
mlflow.log_param('dataset_path', '/mnt/data/train.parquet')

questions.explanation: D is correct because mlflow.log_param() is designed for logging configuration values that define the training run setup — including data paths, feature names, preprocessing flags, and hyperparameters. These appear in the run's params table and can be used to filter runs. B (set_tag) also works technically but tags are for informational/organizational metadata rather than reproducibility configuration. A (log_metric) only accepts numeric values — a string path would fail. C (log_artifact) would copy the entire dataset file into the artifact store, which is expensive and unnecessary when the path reference is sufficient.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.log_param() for run configuration tracking

questions.stem: A model is currently in the 'Staging' stage of the MLflow Model Registry. After successful validation, an ML engineer wants to promote it to 'Production'. Using MlflowClient, which method performs this stage transition?

options.text: MlflowClient().update_model_version('MyModel', version=2, description='Promoted')
MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage('MyModel', version=2, stage='Production')
mlflow.register_model('models:/MyModel/Staging', 'MyModel')
MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag('MyModel', 2, 'stage', 'Production')

questions.explanation: B is correct because MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage() is the dedicated method for moving a model version through the registry lifecycle stages (None → Staging → Production → Archived). A (update_model_version) updates the description field but cannot change the stage. C (register_model) from a stage URI would create a new model version, not transition an existing one. D (set_model_version_tag) sets a metadata tag but has no effect on the official stage — the version would still be in Staging.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — transition_model_version_stage()

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with a custom PythonModel class that wraps a preprocessing pipeline and a CatBoost classifier. The class implements the predict() method. Which MLflow call correctly logs this custom model?

options.text: mlflow.catboost.log_model(model, 'model')
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model')
mlflow.pyfunc.log_model('model', python_model=MyCustomModel(), artifacts={'catboost_model': model_path})
mlflow.log_artifact(model, 'model')

questions.explanation: C is correct because mlflow.pyfunc.log_model() with a python_model argument is the correct way to log a custom PythonModel subclass. The artifacts dict maps logical names to file paths so the predict() method can access them at inference time. A assumes a native CatBoost MLflow flavor exists in the standard library (it requires mlflow-catboost plugin and still would not wrap custom classes). B (sklearn) only serializes sklearn-compatible objects and does not support custom predict() logic. D (log_artifact) would try to serialize a Python class object as a file, which fails.
questions.reference: MLflow pyfunc — mlflow.pyfunc.log_model() with custom PythonModel

questions.stem: An ML team wants to ensure that any client application loading their model always gets the latest 'Production' version without needing to update model version numbers in client code. Which URI pattern achieves this?

options.text: models:/MyModel/3 (hardcoding the current production version number)
models:/MyModel/Production (classic registry stage-based URI)
runs:/run_id/artifacts/my_model
file:///mlflow/models/MyModel

questions.explanation: B is correct because the stage-based URI models:/ModelName/Production always resolves to whichever version is currently in the Production stage, making client code immune to version number changes when a new version is promoted. A requires updating every client application when a new version is promoted. C uses a run-specific artifact URI that is tied to a specific training run, not a promoted registry version. D is an invalid URI format for the MLflow registry.
questions.reference: MLflow Model Registry — stage-based URI for decoupled client loading

questions.stem: A Feature Store team is designing feature tables for a recommendation system. Select TWO properties that must be defined when calling FeatureStoreClient.create_table().

options.text: name — the full three-part table name (catalog.schema.table)
primary_keys — the column(s) that uniquely identify each entity
partition_by — the column to partition the feature table storage by
schema — the DataFrame schema defining feature column names and types

questions.explanation: A is correct because the table name (fully qualified) is a required parameter for create_table(). B is correct because primary_keys is required — it defines the entity key(s) used to look up features during training set creation and batch scoring. Without primary_keys, the Feature Store cannot perform joins. C (partition_by) is optional and used for storage optimization, not required for table creation. D (schema) — while the schema is needed, it can be inferred from a provided DataFrame via the df parameter or specified via schema; the core required parameters are name and primary_keys.
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — create_table() required parameters

questions.stem: A data scientist writes a feature pipeline that calls fs.write_table() with mode='merge'. What is the behavior of 'merge' mode in the Databricks Feature Store?

options.text: Overwrites the entire feature table with the new DataFrame
Appends all rows from the new DataFrame without deduplication
Upserts rows — inserting new entity keys and updating existing entity keys with new feature values
Creates a new version of the feature table without modifying the existing data

questions.explanation: C is correct because mode='merge' in FeatureStoreClient.write_table() performs an upsert operation: rows with primary keys that already exist in the feature table are updated with new values, and rows with new primary keys are inserted. This is the standard pattern for incremental feature updates. A describes mode='overwrite'. B describes mode='append' (which does allow duplicate primary keys). D is not a valid write_table mode — feature tables do not have versioning in the write_table interface.
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — write_table() merge mode (upsert)

questions.stem: An ML engineer needs to find all runs in an experiment where val_f1 > 0.85 and the regularization parameter alpha is less than 0.01. Using MlflowClient, which filter_string should they use?

options.text: "metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 OR params.alpha < 0.01"
"metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 AND params.alpha < '0.01'"
"val_f1 > 0.85 AND alpha < 0.01"
"metrics.val_f1 > 0.85 AND params.alpha < 0.01"

questions.explanation: D is correct because MLflow search filter strings require the namespace prefix — metrics. for metric values and params. for parameter values. The AND operator combines conditions, and numeric comparisons work directly for metrics. Note that params values are stored as strings in MLflow, so numeric comparisons on params may not always work as expected, but this is the syntactically correct filter string. A uses OR instead of AND. B wraps the param value in quotes, making it a string comparison which breaks numeric ordering. C omits the required metrics. and params. namespace prefixes.
questions.reference: MlflowClient.search_runs() — filter_string syntax

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to add a human-readable description to a registered model version explaining the changes made. Which MlflowClient call accomplishes this?

options.text: MlflowClient().set_model_version_tag('MyModel', 2, 'description', 'Improved recall')
MlflowClient().update_model_version('MyModel', version=2, description='Improved recall with new training data')
MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage('MyModel', 2, stage='Production', archive_existing_versions=True)
mlflow.set_tag('model_description', 'Improved recall with new training data')

questions.explanation: B is correct because MlflowClient().update_model_version() is the dedicated method for updating the description field on a specific model version in the registry. The description appears in the Model Registry UI. A (set_model_version_tag) adds a tag key-value pair to the version's metadata, which is searchable, but 'description' as a tag is not the same as the official description field. C (transition_model_version_stage) changes the stage, not the description. D (set_tag) sets a tag on the active training run, not on a registered model version.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — update_model_version() for version descriptions

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with mlflow.sklearn.log_model() but then discovers the model was logged without a model signature. Without retraining, how can they add a signature to the existing registered model version?

options.text: Call mlflow.register_model() again with a signature argument to overwrite the version
Download the model artifacts, add the signature using mlflow.models.set_signature(), then re-upload the artifacts
Signatures can only be added at training time — the model must be retrained
Set a tag on the model version with key 'signature' containing the JSON schema

questions.explanation: B is correct because MLflow provides mlflow.models.set_signature() which modifies the signature of an existing logged model in-place in the artifact store. The workflow is: download the model artifacts (or reference the run URI), call mlflow.models.set_signature(model_uri, signature) where signature is created via mlflow.models.infer_signature(), which updates the MLmodel file without retraining. A would create a new model version, not update the existing one's signature. C is false — set_signature() exists precisely for this scenario. D is wrong because tags are metadata and are not read by MLflow serving as a model signature.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.models.set_signature() to add signatures post-hoc

questions.stem: A Feature Store table stores daily aggregated user behavior features. An ML team wants to create a training set that joins these features to label events using point-in-time lookups. What additional configuration is required in the FeatureLookup definition?

options.text: Set output_name to specify where to write the joined features
Specify timestamp_lookup_key in the FeatureLookup to indicate which column in the labels DataFrame holds the event timestamp
Provide a join_type='left' argument to handle missing feature values
Set mode='point_in_time' in create_training_set()

questions.explanation: B is correct because to enable point-in-time lookups when calling create_training_set(), each FeatureLookup must specify a timestamp_lookup_key — the column in the observation/labels DataFrame that holds the event timestamp. The Feature Store then retrieves the feature value as of that timestamp, preventing future data leakage. A (output_name) renames the feature column in the output, unrelated to point-in-time behavior. C (join_type) is not a parameter of FeatureLookup — the join behavior is determined by the feature table's timestamp key. D (mode='point_in_time') is not a valid parameter for create_training_set().
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — FeatureLookup timestamp_lookup_key for point-in-time

questions.stem: A data scientist creates a Spark UDF from an MLflow model for batch inference. What is the main difference between using env_manager='conda' versus env_manager='local' in mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf()?

options.text: env_manager='conda' creates a conda environment matching the model's dependencies on each worker, ensuring reproducibility; env_manager='local' uses the existing Python environment on workers
env_manager='conda' runs inference on the driver only; env_manager='local' uses all workers
env_manager='conda' only works with sklearn models; env_manager='local' works with all flavors
env_manager='conda' is required for GPU models; env_manager='local' is for CPU models

questions.explanation: A is correct because env_manager='conda' instructs each worker to create a conda environment from the model's conda.yaml, ensuring the exact library versions used during training are present at inference time. env_manager='local' uses whatever Python environment is already installed on the workers, which is faster to start but risks version mismatches. B is wrong — both options distribute inference across workers. C is wrong — both work with all model flavors. D is wrong — GPU/CPU configuration is unrelated to the env_manager choice.
questions.reference: mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() — env_manager parameter

questions.stem: An ML team wants to serve multiple model versions simultaneously on a Databricks Model Serving endpoint. They configure the endpoint to send 80% of traffic to version 3 and 20% to version 4. After two weeks, version 4 shows higher accuracy. What is the next step in this deployment strategy?

options.text: Delete version 3 from the Model Registry and redeploy the endpoint with only version 4
Update the endpoint's traffic split to 100% version 4 and 0% version 3, then monitor before retiring version 3
Create a new separate endpoint for version 4 and update DNS routing
Retrain version 3 with version 4's hyperparameters to make them equivalent

questions.explanation: B is correct because the standard A/B rollout completion pattern is to gradually shift traffic to the winning version — setting version 4 to 100% and version 3 to 0% within the same endpoint — while keeping version 3 registered and reachable for quick rollback if needed. A is wrong because deleting version 3 eliminates the rollback option. C (new endpoint) is unnecessarily complex and requires client URL updates. D (retraining version 3) is counterproductive — if version 4 is better, the goal is to promote version 4, not equalize them.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — A/B traffic split rollout completion

questions.stem: An ML team wants to score a batch of 100K new customer records using a model that was trained using the Databricks Feature Store, where features are stored in a table with 'customer_id' as the primary key. Which FeatureStoreClient call is correct for batch scoring?

options.text: fs.score_batch('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_features)
fs.score_batch('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_customer_ids)
fs.predict('models:/ChurnModel/Production', df_with_customer_ids)
mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/ChurnModel/Production').predict(df_with_customer_ids)

questions.explanation: B is correct because FeatureStoreClient.score_batch() only requires a DataFrame containing the primary key column(s) (customer_id in this case) plus any non-feature columns. It automatically retrieves the required features from the Feature Store using the model's logged feature lineage metadata. A is wrong because if df_with_features already contains all features, the Feature Store's automatic lookup is bypassed — but more importantly, score_batch() is designed to accept just the keys. C (fs.predict) is not a valid FeatureStoreClient method. D loads the model without Feature Store metadata, so automatic feature lookup is unavailable and the input DataFrame would need to already contain all features.
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — score_batch() with primary key DataFrame

questions.stem: A team is evaluating real-time model serving latency on Databricks. Select TWO factors that most directly increase inference latency on a Model Serving endpoint.

options.text: Cold start time when the endpoint scales from zero to one instance after a period of inactivity
The number of model versions registered in the MLflow Model Registry
Heavy preprocessing or feature transformation logic executed inside the model's predict() method
The number of unused experiments in the MLflow tracking server

questions.explanation: A is correct because when an endpoint scales to zero and receives a new request, it must spin up a container, load the model, and initialize the environment — adding seconds of cold start latency to that first request. C is correct because any preprocessing (e.g., tokenization, complex feature engineering, large dataframe transforms) inside the predict() method executes serially on every request, directly adding to p99 latency. B is wrong because the number of registry versions does not affect serving endpoint latency — the endpoint only loads the served version. D is wrong because unused experiments in the tracking server have no impact on the serving endpoint.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — latency factors: cold start and preprocessing overhead

questions.stem: An ML engineer deploys a Structured Streaming pipeline that uses foreachBatch to apply a fraud detection model to micro-batches. The model is loaded inside the batch function. After running for 24 hours, they notice executor memory pressure. What is the root cause and the fix?

options.text: The model grows in size over time; fix by periodically calling gc.collect() inside the batch function
The model is reloaded into worker memory on every micro-batch, accumulating without being released; fix by loading the model once outside the streaming query and broadcasting it
foreachBatch does not support model inference; migrate to a DataFrame API approach
Streaming checkpoints are storing model weights; fix by disabling checkpointing

questions.explanation: B is correct because when mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() is called inside the foreachBatch function, a new copy of the model is loaded into executor JVM/Python worker memory on every micro-batch invocation. If micro-batches arrive faster than the garbage collector can reclaim the old model objects, memory accumulates, causing pressure. The fix is to load the model once before the streaming query starts and broadcast it using SparkContext.broadcast(), so each executor gets one copy that persists across batches. A is wrong — model weights don't grow dynamically. C is wrong — foreachBatch fully supports model inference. D is wrong — checkpointing stores query state metadata, not model weights.
questions.reference: Structured Streaming — model memory pressure from per-batch loading

questions.stem: A batch inference job uses mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() and runs on a 10-node cluster. The job completes but 3 worker nodes have error logs showing 'ModuleNotFoundError: No module named lightgbm'. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The LightGBM model was not logged correctly in MLflow
The spark_udf() was called with env_manager='local' and lightgbm is not installed on those 3 worker nodes
The model's MLflow flavor is incompatible with the Spark version
The result_type parameter was set incorrectly for a LightGBM model

questions.explanation: B is correct because env_manager='local' (or the default behavior when conda is not enforced) uses the Python environment already installed on each worker. If lightgbm was not installed uniformly on all cluster nodes — a common situation with autoscaled clusters where new nodes may not have all packages — some workers will fail with ModuleNotFoundError. The fix is to use env_manager='conda' to ensure the model's conda.yaml dependencies are installed, or install lightgbm via cluster init scripts. A is wrong — a logging issue would cause a different error at load time. C is wrong — LightGBM and Spark compatibility is a separate concern. D is wrong — result_type mismatch causes type errors, not ModuleNotFoundError.
questions.reference: mlflow.pyfunc.spark_udf() — dependency management with env_manager

questions.stem: An ML engineer converts a scikit-learn model to ONNX for edge deployment. After conversion, they observe that the ONNX model's predictions for some inputs differ slightly from the original sklearn model. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: ONNX conversion applies additional L2 regularization to the model weights
Floating-point precision differences between sklearn's Python/numpy operations and ONNX Runtime's optimized operations
ONNX does not support the model architecture and is silently approximating it
The ONNX Runtime version must exactly match the sklearn version to produce identical results

questions.explanation: B is correct because ONNX Runtime executes computation graphs using highly optimized kernels (often using different floating-point operation ordering than numpy) which can introduce small numerical differences due to floating-point non-associativity. These differences are typically in the 1e-6 to 1e-4 range and are considered acceptable for production. A is wrong — ONNX conversion does not modify model weights. C is wrong — sklearn-onnx (skl2onnx) fully supports standard sklearn estimators and performs exact functional conversion. D is wrong — there is no requirement for version matching between sklearn and ONNX Runtime.
questions.reference: ONNX conversion — floating-point precision differences in ONNX Runtime

questions.stem:
A model is served via a Databricks Model Serving REST endpoint. A developer sends a prediction request using the following curl command:

```bash
curl -X POST https://<workspace>/serving-endpoints/MyModel/invocations \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"dataframe_records": [{"feature1": 1.5, "feature2": 0.3}]}'
```

The endpoint returns a 400 error. The model was logged with a signature requiring feature1 and feature2 as floats. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: The JSON payload format should use 'inputs' instead of 'dataframe_records'
The Authorization header format is incorrect for Databricks endpoints
The feature1 value 1.5 exceeds the maximum allowed float value
The endpoint name 'MyModel' cannot contain uppercase letters

questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks Model Serving (backed by MLflow serving) supports two payload formats: 'dataframe_split' (with columns and data keys) and 'dataframe_records' (list of dicts). However, some MLflow model server versions expect 'inputs' as the outer key for tensor-based inputs. If the endpoint is configured for the 'inputs' format (common for non-pyfunc models or specific signature types), 'dataframe_records' would return a 400. The correct format for pyfunc models is typically {'dataframe_records': [...]} or {'dataframe_split': {'columns': [...], 'data': [...]}}. B is wrong because 'Bearer <token>' is the correct Databricks auth format. C is wrong because there is no maximum float cap. D is wrong because endpoint names can contain uppercase letters.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — REST API payload formats

questions.stem: An ML team wants to detect drift in a categorical feature (e.g., 'product_category' with 15 possible values). Which statistical test is most appropriate?

options.text: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test
Chi-squared goodness-of-fit test
T-test for independent samples
Mann-Whitney U test

questions.explanation: B is correct because the chi-squared goodness-of-fit test compares the observed frequency distribution of categorical values in production against the expected distribution from training, making it the standard choice for categorical drift detection. A (KS test) is for continuous numerical distributions, not categorical. C (T-test) compares means of continuous variables between two groups, not categorical distributions. D (Mann-Whitney U) is a non-parametric test for comparing ranks of continuous distributions, not categorical counts.
questions.reference: Drift Detection — chi-squared test for categorical features

questions.stem: A production ML model's Population Stability Index (PSI) for a key feature is calculated as 0.28. How should the monitoring team interpret this value and what action should they take?

options.text: PSI < 0.1 means no shift; 0.1-0.25 means moderate shift; PSI > 0.25 means significant shift requiring investigation. PSI=0.28 signals significant shift — trigger model review and potential retraining.
PSI is on a 0-1 scale; 0.28 means 28% of the feature distribution has shifted, which is within the acceptable 30% threshold.
PSI > 0 always requires immediate model retraining regardless of magnitude.
PSI of 0.28 indicates 72% model accuracy on the shifted distribution.

questions.explanation: A is correct because the standard PSI interpretation thresholds are: PSI < 0.1 (no significant shift), 0.1-0.25 (moderate shift, monitor closely), PSI > 0.25 (significant shift, investigate and consider retraining). A PSI of 0.28 exceeds the 0.25 threshold, signaling significant distribution shift that warrants model review. B is wrong because PSI is not a percentage scale — it has no upper bound and values above 0.25 indicate significant shift regardless of whether 0.30 is also significant. C is wrong because minor PSI values (< 0.1) do not require retraining. D is wrong — PSI has no relationship to accuracy percentage.
questions.reference: Monitoring — PSI thresholds: <0.1 stable, 0.1-0.25 moderate, >0.25 significant

questions.stem: A Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring job is configured on an inference table that stores model predictions and ground truth labels. What type of monitor should the team configure to track model quality metrics (precision, recall) over time?

options.text: TimeSeries monitor
InferenceLog monitor
Snapshot monitor
Profile monitor

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring's InferenceLog monitor type is specifically designed for tables that contain model predictions alongside ground truth labels. It computes model quality metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, RMSE, etc.) over time windows and detects drift in both the prediction distribution and model performance. A (TimeSeries) is for tables with a time dimension tracking non-ML metrics. C (Snapshot) computes statistics for a single point-in-time profile of a table. D (Profile) generates statistical summaries but is not the specific type for inference log analysis with ground truth.
questions.reference: Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring — InferenceLog monitor for model quality

questions.stem: An ML team is designing an automated retraining pipeline that triggers when model performance degrades in production. Select TWO signals that would be appropriate triggers for automated retraining.

options.text: The 7-day rolling F1 score on labeled production data drops below 0.80, compared to a baseline of 0.87 at deployment
The number of prediction requests to the serving endpoint exceeds 10,000 per hour
The KS test p-value for a key numerical feature drops below 0.05 for three consecutive monitoring windows
A new version of the ML framework (e.g., scikit-learn) is released

questions.explanation: A is correct because a statistically significant and sustained drop in F1 score against ground truth labels is a direct indicator of model performance degradation — the clearest trigger for retraining. C is correct because sustained input feature drift (KS p-value < 0.05 = statistically significant distribution shift) for three consecutive windows suggests the data generating process has changed enough that the current model is likely making suboptimal predictions, warranting retraining on new data. B (request volume) indicates traffic patterns, not model degradation — high volume is a scaling concern, not a retraining trigger. D (framework updates) is a dependency management concern, not a model performance trigger.
questions.reference: Monitoring — retraining triggers: F1 degradation and sustained drift detection

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.active_run() inside a training function. Under which condition does this return None?

options.text: When called before the first training run has ever been started in the session
When mlflow.autolog() is enabled — autolog disables the active run context
When the MLflow tracking server is not reachable
When the function is called on a worker node rather than the driver

questions.explanation: A is correct because mlflow.active_run() returns the currently active Run object if a run is open (via mlflow.start_run() or autolog), and returns None when no run is currently active — such as when called outside any run context or before any run has been started. B is wrong — autolog opens a run automatically, so active_run() would return that run. C is wrong — network connectivity affects logging calls but active_run() reads local Python state, not the server. D is wrong — active_run() operates on driver-side Python state; it is not typically called inside Spark worker functions.
questions.reference: MLflow Tracking — mlflow.active_run() returning None when no run is active

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.evaluate() with a custom metric function. They define `def my_metric(eval_df, builtin_metrics): return MetricValue(aggregate_results={'my_score': 0.92})`. Which parameter of mlflow.evaluate() accepts this function?

options.text: extra_metrics=[make_metric(eval_fn=my_metric, greater_is_better=True)]
custom_metrics=[my_metric]
metric_functions={'my_metric': my_metric}
evaluators=[my_metric]

questions.explanation: A is correct because mlflow.evaluate() accepts custom metric functions through the extra_metrics parameter, which takes a list of EvaluationMetric objects created with mlflow.models.make_metric(). make_metric() wraps the custom function and requires specifying greater_is_better to determine optimality direction. B is wrong because custom_metrics is not the correct parameter name for mlflow.evaluate(). C is wrong because no dict-based metric_functions parameter exists. D is wrong because evaluators specifies which evaluator backend to use (e.g., 'default', 'shap'), not custom metric functions.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.evaluate() extra_metrics with make_metric() for custom metrics

questions.stem: An ML team wants to share an MLflow experiment between two Databricks workspaces in the same account. A data scientist in workspace B needs read access to runs created in workspace A. What is the correct approach in Databricks?

options.text: Export the experiment as a JSON file from workspace A and import it into workspace B
Move all experiments to a Unity Catalog-backed MLflow tracking server accessible to both workspaces
Duplicate the runs by re-logging all metrics and artifacts in workspace B
Change the tracking URI in workspace B to point to workspace A's tracking server

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks Unity Catalog provides a unified governance layer across workspaces. When MLflow experiments are stored in a Unity Catalog-enabled metastore shared across workspaces, users in both workspaces can access the same experiments and runs with appropriate permissions. This is the supported cross-workspace experiment sharing pattern. A (JSON export/import) is a migration technique, not a live sharing mechanism. C (duplicating runs) duplicates data and creates synchronization problems. D (changing tracking URI) is not a supported configuration for cross-workspace access — each workspace's MLflow tracking server is workspace-scoped unless using Unity Catalog.
questions.reference: Databricks MLflow — cross-workspace experiment sharing via Unity Catalog

questions.stem: An ML engineer runs nested runs for an ensemble model: a parent run for the ensemble and child runs for each base learner. They call mlflow.start_run(nested=True) for each child. After all child runs finish, where should the ensemble model artifact be logged?

options.text: To one of the child runs that performed best, so the best base learner is promoted
To the parent run, since the ensemble represents the combined outcome of all base learners
To a separate new run created after the nested runs complete
To all child runs simultaneously using mlflow.log_artifact() with a shared artifact path

questions.explanation: B is correct because in the nested run pattern for ensembles, each child run represents one component (base learner), while the parent run represents the overall experiment. The final ensemble model — which combines outputs of all base learners — should be logged to the parent run so that the artifact URI and model registration are associated with the ensemble entity, not any individual component. A is wrong because logging the ensemble to a child run conflates the component with the aggregate result. C creates unnecessary run fragmentation. D is technically possible but violates the purpose of nesting — the ensemble belongs to the parent.
questions.reference: MLflow — nested runs for ensembles: logging the ensemble model to the parent run

questions.stem: A data scientist defines a HyperOpt search space with hp.quniform('batch_size', 32, 512, 32). What values can this expression produce?

options.text: Any integer between 32 and 512, uniformly sampled
Multiples of 32 between 32 and 512: [32, 64, 96, ..., 512]
Exactly 32 or 512 with equal probability
A float uniformly sampled from (32, 512) then quantized to the nearest integer

questions.explanation: B is correct because hp.quniform(label, low, high, q) samples from round(uniform(low, high) / q) * q, producing values that are multiples of q within the [low, high] range. With q=32, the possible values are 32, 64, 96, ..., 512 — exactly the common batch size choices that are powers of 2 or multiples of 32, which is a practical hyperparameter constraint. A is wrong — quniform is quantized, not arbitrary integers. C is wrong — only the endpoints are not guaranteed; all multiples of q are sampled with roughly equal probability. D is wrong — the output is quantized to multiples of q, not to the nearest integer.
questions.reference: HyperOpt — hp.quniform for quantized hyperparameters like batch size

questions.stem: A data scientist calls mlflow.log_artifact('/tmp/model_card.html', artifact_path='documentation') and mlflow.log_artifacts('/tmp/plots/', artifact_path='visualizations') within the same run. Select TWO true statements about where these files are stored in the artifact store.

options.text: model_card.html will be stored at 'documentation/model_card.html' relative to the run's artifact root
All files inside /tmp/plots/ will be stored at 'visualizations/<filename>' relative to the run's artifact root
log_artifacts() with artifact_path will ignore subdirectories inside /tmp/plots/
Both calls write to the same artifact root directory, overwriting each other if filenames clash

questions.explanation: A is correct because mlflow.log_artifact(local_path, artifact_path) stores the single file at '<artifact_path>/<filename>' — so model_card.html lands at 'documentation/model_card.html' in the artifact store. B is correct because mlflow.log_artifacts(local_dir, artifact_path) recursively uploads all files in the local directory, placing each at '<artifact_path>/<relative_file_path>' — so every file in /tmp/plots/ lands under 'visualizations/'. C is wrong — log_artifacts() does preserve directory structure for subdirectories. D is wrong — the artifact_path parameters 'documentation' and 'visualizations' are different namespaces, so they cannot overwrite each other.
questions.reference: MLflow — log_artifact vs log_artifacts artifact_path behavior

questions.stem: A data scientist wants the default artifact storage location for new MLflow experiments to be a specific DBFS path. Which MLflow function sets this at experiment creation time?

options.text: mlflow.set_tracking_uri('dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts')
mlflow.create_experiment('MyExp', artifact_location='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')
mlflow.set_experiment('MyExp', artifact_location='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')
mlflow.log_artifact('/dev/null', artifact_path='dbfs:/mlflow/artifacts/MyExp')

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.create_experiment() accepts an optional artifact_location parameter that sets the default root URI where all run artifacts for that experiment are stored. This must be set at creation time; it cannot be changed after the experiment is created. A (set_tracking_uri) sets where the MLflow tracking server is located, not where artifacts are stored within an experiment. C is wrong because mlflow.set_experiment() does not accept an artifact_location parameter — it just sets the active experiment by name. D is wrong because log_artifact is for logging a file, not configuring storage location.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.create_experiment() artifact_location for custom artifact root

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.log_input() to log a training dataset with source='dbfs:/data/train.delta'. Later, an auditor queries the dataset inputs for model version 7 of 'RiskModel'. Which sequence of calls retrieves this information?

options.text: client.get_registered_model('RiskModel').dataset_inputs
client.get_model_version('RiskModel', '7').dataset_inputs
version = client.get_model_version('RiskModel', '7'); run = client.get_run(version.run_id); run.inputs.dataset_inputs
mlflow.search_runs(filter_string="tags.dataset_source='dbfs:/data/train.delta'")

questions.explanation: C is correct because dataset inputs logged with mlflow.log_input() are attached to the training run as RunInputs. To retrieve them for a registered model version: (1) get the model version to find its run_id, (2) get the run using that run_id, (3) access run.inputs.dataset_inputs which is a list of DatasetInput objects containing the source URI, schema, and profile. A is wrong because registered model objects do not have dataset_inputs. B is wrong because model version objects also do not have dataset_inputs. D partially works for finding runs but does not retrieve structured DatasetInput objects.
questions.reference: MLflow — retrieving dataset lineage: model version → run_id → run.inputs.dataset_inputs

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to log both training and validation metrics to a single MLflow run using a single function call. They have a dict {'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94}. Which call logs all three at once?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('train_loss', 0.12); mlflow.log_metric('val_loss', 0.18); mlflow.log_metric('val_acc', 0.94)
mlflow.log_metrics({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})
mlflow.log_params({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})
mlflow.set_tags({'train_loss': 0.12, 'val_loss': 0.18, 'val_acc': 0.94})

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.log_metrics() (plural) accepts a dict of metric_name → float_value pairs and logs all of them in a single API call, which is more efficient than individual log_metric() calls. A works but makes three separate API calls. C (log_params) stores these as hyperparameter strings, not as numeric metrics — they would appear in the params panel, not the metrics chart. D (set_tags) stores them as string tags, not numeric metrics.
questions.reference: MLflow — mlflow.log_metrics() for batch metric logging

questions.stem: An MLOps engineer needs to set up an automated notification to a Slack channel whenever a model version transitions to the Staging stage in the MLflow Model Registry. Which MLflow registry webhook event should they use?

options.text: MODEL_VERSION_CREATED
TRANSITION_REQUEST_CREATED
MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE
REGISTERED_MODEL_CREATED

questions.explanation: C is correct because MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE fires when a model version's stage is changed (e.g., to Staging or Production). This is the correct event for triggering notifications after a stage transition completes. A (MODEL_VERSION_CREATED) fires when a new version is first registered, before any stage assignment. B (TRANSITION_REQUEST_CREATED) fires when a transition is requested but not yet approved — useful for approval workflows, not post-transition notifications. D (REGISTERED_MODEL_CREATED) fires when a new model name is first registered, not on version transitions.
questions.reference: MLflow Registry Webhooks — MODEL_VERSION_TRANSITIONED_STAGE event

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a model with mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, 'model', extra_pip_requirements=['shap==0.42.0', 'pandas>=1.3']). How are these extra requirements stored in the model artifact?

options.text: They replace the auto-generated conda.yaml entirely with only the specified packages
They are appended to the model's requirements.txt file, which is used alongside conda.yaml at serving time
They are stored as a run tag with key 'extra_pip_requirements'
They override the conda.yaml by replacing the pip section with only the extra requirements

questions.explanation: B is correct because extra_pip_requirements are appended to the model's requirements.txt file (not the conda.yaml). The requirements.txt is generated alongside conda.yaml in the model artifact directory. MLflow serving environments install both conda.yaml dependencies and requirements.txt dependencies. The extra_pip_requirements are additive — they do not replace the auto-detected dependencies. A is wrong because the conda.yaml is auto-generated with all detected packages plus extra ones. C is wrong — requirements are stored in the model artifact, not run tags. D is wrong — extra_pip_requirements are appended to requirements.txt, not used to override the conda.yaml pip section.
questions.reference: MLflow log_model — extra_pip_requirements appended to requirements.txt

questions.stem: A Unity Catalog model 'prod.ml.ChurnPredictor' has version 5 tagged as 'champion'. A new version 6 is trained and passes validation. The team wants to atomically promote version 6 to champion and demote version 5 to challenger without a gap. Which set of calls achieves this?

options.text: client.delete_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion'); client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion', 6)
client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'champion', 6); client.set_registered_model_alias('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 'challenger', 5)
client.transition_model_version_stage('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 5, 'Archived'); client.transition_model_version_stage('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', 6, 'Production')
client.update_model_version('prod.ml.ChurnPredictor', version=6, alias='champion')

questions.explanation: B is correct because in the Unity Catalog registry, set_registered_model_alias() reassigns an alias to a new version. When you call set_registered_model_alias('...', 'champion', 6), the alias 'champion' is atomically moved from version 5 to version 6 — there is no gap because the alias always points to exactly one version. The second call assigns the 'challenger' alias to version 5. A is wrong because deleting the alias first creates a gap where no champion exists. C is wrong because transition_model_version_stage() is a classic registry method, not available in Unity Catalog (which uses aliases instead of stages). D is wrong because update_model_version() has no alias parameter.
questions.reference: Unity Catalog Registry — set_registered_model_alias() for atomic alias reassignment

questions.stem: A data science team is evaluating using Databricks Feature Store vs. manual feature engineering for a production ML pipeline. Select TWO advantages of using the Feature Store's log_model() and score_batch() workflow over manual Spark joins + spark_udf().

options.text: The model artifact records which feature tables it depends on, enabling automatic feature retrieval at inference time from just entity keys
Feature Store log_model() automatically performs hyperparameter tuning before serializing the model
Eliminates the need to write and maintain join logic between entity tables and feature tables in the inference pipeline
Feature Store models are automatically deployed to a Model Serving endpoint upon registration

questions.explanation: A is correct because FeatureStoreClient.log_model() records the feature lineage (which tables, which keys, which columns) as model metadata. At inference time, score_batch() uses this metadata to automatically fetch the correct features — the caller only provides entity primary keys. C is correct because this lineage-driven auto-lookup eliminates manual join code in inference pipelines, reducing maintenance burden and preventing join logic drift between training and serving. B is wrong — Feature Store has no hyperparameter optimization capability. D is wrong — Feature Store model registration does not automatically create a serving endpoint; that is a separate Databricks Model Serving action.
questions.reference: Databricks Feature Store — log_model() and score_batch() vs manual join pattern

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to search for all registered model versions of 'SalesModel' that are currently in the 'Production' stage. Which filter_string correctly retrieves them?

options.text: MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='SalesModel' AND stage='Production'")
MlflowClient().get_latest_versions('SalesModel', stages=['Production'])
MlflowClient().search_model_versions("name='SalesModel'") and then filter client-side by version.current_stage == 'Production'
MlflowClient().list_model_versions('SalesModel', stage='Production')

questions.explanation: A is correct because search_model_versions() supports filtering by 'stage' as a filter attribute in the filter string, allowing server-side filtering. This is the most efficient approach. B (get_latest_versions) returns only the latest version per requested stage, not all versions that have ever been in Production — for models with multiple versions, this may not return all desired versions. C works but is inefficient because it retrieves all versions and filters client-side. D uses a non-existent list_model_versions() method.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — search_model_versions() with stage filter

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to programmatically retrieve the artifact URI of a specific model version so they can inspect the raw model files. Given version = client.get_model_version('MyModel', '3'), which attribute contains the artifact URI?

options.text: version.artifact_uri
version.source
version.run_artifact_uri
version.model_uri

questions.explanation: B is correct because the ModelVersion object returned by get_model_version() stores the artifact location in the 'source' attribute. This is the runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path> URI pointing to the original logged model artifact in the run's artifact store. This can be passed to mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts() or used to construct the model loading URI. A (artifact_uri) is not a standard attribute on ModelVersion. C (run_artifact_uri) does not exist. D (model_uri) is not a ModelVersion attribute — the models:/ URI is constructed from the model name and version, not stored as a field.
questions.reference: MLflow ModelVersion — version.source attribute for artifact URI

questions.stem: An ML team uses Databricks Feature Store to build training datasets. They have a label DataFrame with columns ['user_id', 'churn_label', 'event_ts'] and a feature table 'catalog.features.user_activity' with primary key 'user_id' and timestamp key 'activity_ts'. Which FeatureLookup correctly performs point-in-time lookup using event_ts from the label DataFrame?

options.text: FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id')
FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id', timestamp_lookup_key='event_ts')
FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', feature_names=['clicks_7d', 'sessions_30d'], lookup_key='user_id', timestamp_lookup_key='activity_ts')
FeatureLookup(table_name='catalog.features.user_activity', lookup_key='user_id', point_in_time=True)

questions.explanation: B is correct because timestamp_lookup_key must reference the column in the label/observation DataFrame (not the feature table) that holds the event timestamp. In this case, 'event_ts' is the observation time in the label DataFrame, and the Feature Store will look up feature values from 'user_activity' as of each row's 'event_ts'. A is wrong because it omits timestamp_lookup_key, disabling point-in-time correctness. C is wrong because 'activity_ts' is the feature table's own timestamp column, not the label DataFrame's event timestamp — timestamp_lookup_key must reference the observation time in the label data. D is wrong because point_in_time is not a valid FeatureLookup parameter.
questions.reference: Feature Store FeatureLookup — timestamp_lookup_key references label DataFrame timestamp

questions.stem: After registering a model, an ML engineer uses mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts(run_id='abc123', artifact_path='model') to retrieve the model files. What is the local directory structure that results from this call?

options.text: A single file named 'model' is downloaded to the current working directory
The full artifact directory at 'model' path in run abc123 is downloaded, including MLmodel file, conda.yaml, python_env.yaml, and the model weights
Only the MLmodel manifest file is downloaded; model weights require a separate call
The download fails because artifact_path must be a full URI, not a relative path

questions.explanation: B is correct because mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts() with a run_id and artifact_path downloads the entire artifact subtree at that path. MLflow model artifacts are directories containing: the MLmodel manifest, conda.yaml, python_env.yaml, requirements.txt, and the serialized model weights (e.g., model.pkl for sklearn, model.pt for PyTorch). The entire directory is downloaded recursively. A is wrong — models are directories, not single files. C is wrong — the full directory is downloaded, not just the manifest. D is wrong — artifact_path is a relative path within the run's artifact store, which is a valid and required input format.
questions.reference: mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts() — full model directory download

questions.stem: A model governance policy requires all Production models in the classic registry to have a non-None description AND a tag 'approved_by' set to a reviewer name. A data scientist attempts to promote a version without these. What is the programmatic enforcement pattern?

options.text: Configure a registry webhook that blocks the stage transition if governance checks fail
Before calling transition_model_version_stage(), check version.description is not None and 'approved_by' in client.get_model_version('M', v).tags; raise an error if checks fail
Set a registry-level policy in the Databricks workspace settings that enforces tags before transitions
Use mlflow.validate_model_version() to automatically enforce governance policies

questions.explanation: B is correct because programmatic governance gates are implemented as pre-condition checks in code before calling transition_model_version_stage(). The pattern is: (1) retrieve the version object, (2) check version.description is populated, (3) check that 'approved_by' exists in the version's tags (accessible via client.get_model_version_tags() or the tags attribute), and (4) only call transition if all checks pass. This is the standard MLOps governance pattern. A (registry webhooks) can notify after events but cannot block synchronous stage transitions. C is wrong — no such workspace-level policy enforcement exists in MLflow. D is wrong — mlflow.validate_model_version() is not a valid MLflow function.
questions.reference: MlflowClient — programmatic governance gates before transition_model_version_stage()

questions.stem: An ML team wants to deploy a new version of their model to Databricks Model Serving with zero downtime. They currently have version 3 serving 100% of traffic. What is the recommended deployment sequence?

options.text: Take the endpoint offline, update it to serve version 4, then bring it back online
Configure the endpoint to serve both version 3 and version 4 simultaneously with a traffic split (e.g., 90%/10%), then gradually shift to 100% version 4
Delete version 3 from the registry and register version 4 with the same version number
Create a new endpoint for version 4 and migrate clients by updating their base URL

questions.explanation: B is correct because Databricks Model Serving supports in-place traffic splitting — adding a new model version to an existing endpoint with a portion of traffic allows zero-downtime deployment. The existing version continues serving its share of traffic while the new version is tested. Once confident, traffic is gradually shifted to 100% new version. A causes downtime during the update window. C is wrong because version numbers are immutable in MLflow — you cannot reuse the same version number. D requires client URL changes and creates infrastructure complexity.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — zero-downtime deployment with traffic splitting

questions.stem: An ML engineer calls mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client('databricks') and then uses client.create_endpoint() to deploy a model. What is the primary advantage of using the MLflow Deployments client over configuring the endpoint via the Databricks UI?

options.text: The MLflow Deployments client applies automatic traffic splitting between all registered model versions
Endpoint creation and configuration become code that can be version-controlled, code-reviewed, and automated in CI/CD pipelines
The MLflow Deployments client uses a faster internal API that bypasses Databricks rate limits
The MLflow Deployments client automatically selects the optimal instance type for the model

questions.explanation: B is correct because using the MLflow Deployments API (mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client) to create and configure endpoints makes infrastructure-as-code possible — endpoint configurations live in Python scripts or CI/CD pipelines, can be reviewed, tested, and reproduced across environments. This is the fundamental advantage of API-driven infrastructure over UI-based configuration. A is wrong — automatic traffic splitting is not a feature of the deployments client; traffic config is explicit. C is wrong — the deployments client uses the same Databricks REST API as the UI. D is wrong — instance type selection is explicit in the endpoint configuration.
questions.reference: MLflow Deployments — mlflow.deployments.get_deploy_client('databricks') for IaC

questions.stem: An ML engineer is deciding whether to use GPU instances for a Databricks Model Serving endpoint. The model is a 100-parameter logistic regression. Which statement best describes when GPU instances are cost-effective for Model Serving?

options.text: GPU instances are always faster than CPU and should be used for any production model

GPU instances are cost-effective for large deep learning models (millions+ of parameters, e.g., transformer-based NLP or image models) where GPU parallelism provides substantial throughput gains over CPU
GPU instances are required for any model trained using PyTorch or TensorFlow
GPU instances should be used when the serving endpoint has more than 100 concurrent requests per second

questions.explanation: B is correct because GPU acceleration provides the greatest benefit for large deep learning models with many matrix multiplications that can be parallelized on GPU cores. For a 100-parameter logistic regression, the computation is trivially fast on CPU — GPU overhead (data transfer, kernel launch) would actually add latency at higher cost. GPUs are cost-effective for transformer models, large neural networks, and image models where batch matrix operations saturate GPU compute. A is wrong — for small models, CPU is faster due to lower overhead. C is wrong — PyTorch/TensorFlow models can serve efficiently on CPU if they are small. D is wrong — high concurrency benefits from auto-scaling replicas, not necessarily GPU.
questions.reference: Model Serving — GPU vs CPU: cost-effectiveness for deep learning vs traditional ML

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to perform shadow deployment: the production model (v5) handles all live traffic and returns responses, while a shadow model (v6) receives a copy of each request but its predictions are discarded. What is the purpose of shadow deployment?

options.text: To reduce serving costs by sharing compute between v5 and v6
To test v6 under real production traffic patterns — including edge cases and load levels — without any risk of affecting production outputs
To allow v6 to learn from v5's predictions via online learning
To automatically switch to v6 if v5 exceeds its latency SLA

questions.explanation: B is correct because shadow deployment (also called shadow mode or dark launch) is specifically designed to evaluate a new model version against real production traffic without affecting production responses. v6 receives the same input as v5, generates predictions, and those predictions are logged for offline analysis. This allows the team to compare v6's behavior against real-world data distributions and edge cases that may not be present in test sets. A is wrong — shadow deployment actually increases cost because both models process each request. C is wrong — shadow deployment does not involve online learning. D is wrong — shadow deployment does not implement automatic failover.
questions.reference: Model Deployment — shadow deployment for risk-free production traffic evaluation

questions.stem: An ML engineer is implementing a pyfunc PythonModel that wraps a text preprocessing step (tokenization) and a BERT classification model. Select TWO best practices for the predict() method implementation.

options.text: Load the BERT model and tokenizer in predict() on each call to ensure the latest weights are used
Load the BERT model and tokenizer in load_context() once at model load time, storing them as instance attributes
Accept both pandas DataFrame and numpy array inputs in predict() by checking isinstance(model_input, pd.DataFrame)
Return predictions as a plain Python list to maximize compatibility with downstream consumers

questions.explanation: B is correct because load_context() is called once when the model is loaded for serving, making it the correct place to initialize expensive objects like BERT models and tokenizers. Storing them as self.bert_model and self.tokenizer avoids re-initialization on every request. A is wrong because loading BERT in predict() on every call would add seconds of latency per request and repeatedly allocate GPU memory. C is correct because production pyfunc models receive inputs in different formats depending on the calling context (pandas from Spark UDF, dict from REST endpoint) — defensive type checking enables the model to handle both. D is wrong — returning a pandas Series or DataFrame is preferred for type safety, indexing, and downstream compatibility with spark_udf result_type expectations.
questions.reference: MLflow pyfunc — load_context() for initialization, flexible predict() input handling

questions.stem: A Databricks Model Serving endpoint receives 10,000 requests per minute at peak. The endpoint is configured with provisioned concurrency of 10. Each request takes ~50ms. How many requests will queue (not be immediately served) at peak load?

options.text: None — the endpoint auto-scales instantly to handle any load
Approximately 10,000 * 0.050/60 = 8.3 concurrent requests needed; with concurrency 10, all requests are served with headroom
All 10,000 requests queue because provisioned concurrency only handles one request at a time
10,000 - 10 = 9,990 requests queue because only 10 can be served simultaneously

questions.explanation: B is correct. At 10,000 requests per minute with 50ms per request, the required concurrency is (10,000 req/min) × (50ms/req) × (1 min/60,000ms) ≈ 8.3 concurrent requests. With provisioned concurrency of 10, the endpoint has sufficient capacity (10 > 8.3) to handle peak load without queuing, leaving ~1.7 slots of headroom. A is wrong — auto-scaling is not instant; provisioned concurrency is what handles immediate load. C is wrong — provisioned concurrency of 10 means 10 simultaneous requests, not one at a time. D is wrong — the queue depth is not simply total_requests minus concurrency; it depends on arrival rate vs. service rate.
questions.reference: Databricks Model Serving — provisioned concurrency and throughput calculation

questions.stem: A data science team builds a custom monitoring solution using Evidently AI to generate data drift reports. Compared to Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring, what is the primary tradeoff of this approach?

options.text: Evidently AI reports are less accurate than Lakehouse Monitoring statistical tests
Custom Evidently AI monitoring requires the team to build and maintain orchestration, storage, and alerting infrastructure that Lakehouse Monitoring provides out-of-the-box
Evidently AI does not support numerical feature drift detection
Evidently AI reports cannot be scheduled and must be run manually

questions.explanation: B is correct because the primary tradeoff of custom monitoring frameworks like Evidently AI vs. managed solutions like Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring is the infrastructure burden. Evidently AI provides rich drift reports and visualizations, but the team must integrate it with a job scheduler, a data store for drift metrics, a dashboard tool, and an alerting system. Lakehouse Monitoring provides a fully managed end-to-end pipeline with Delta table outputs, scheduled refresh, and built-in integration with Databricks SQL dashboards. A is wrong — Evidently AI's statistical tests are rigorous and often more customizable. C is wrong — Evidently AI supports numerical, categorical, and text drift. D is wrong — Evidently AI can be scheduled via any orchestrator.
questions.reference: Monitoring — Evidently AI vs Lakehouse Monitoring: custom vs managed infrastructure

questions.stem: A monitoring team runs a KS test on a revenue feature: from scipy.stats import ks_2samp; stat, p = ks_2samp(train_values, prod_values). The result is stat=0.04, p=0.62. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: The large KS statistic of 0.04 relative to the p-value indicates significant drift
The p-value of 0.62 is well above the 0.05 significance threshold, meaning we fail to reject the null hypothesis — there is no statistically significant evidence of drift
A p-value of 0.62 means 62% of the distribution has drifted
The KS statistic of 0.04 confirms exactly 4% drift in the feature values

questions.explanation: B is correct because a p-value of 0.62 means that under the null hypothesis (the two samples come from the same distribution), there is a 62% probability of observing a KS statistic as large as 0.04 by chance alone. Since 0.62 >> 0.05 (the common significance level), we fail to reject the null hypothesis — the evidence does not support concluding there is distributional drift. A is wrong — 0.04 is a small KS statistic (max possible is 1.0); the interpretation must use the p-value. C is wrong — p-values are not percentages of drift. D is wrong — the KS statistic measures the maximum absolute difference between CDFs, not a percentage of feature values that drifted.
questions.reference: Drift Detection — ks_2samp: high p-value means no statistically significant drift

questions.stem: A production recommendation model has a 14-day ground truth latency (user purchases are recorded with a 2-week delay). The team wants to monitor model quality in near-real-time. Which approach best addresses this latency constraint?

options.text: Wait 14 days before computing any monitoring metrics — accuracy cannot be estimated without true labels
Use prediction distribution monitoring as a leading indicator of model health, and compute true accuracy metrics on a 14-day rolling lag when labels become available
Retrain the model every 14 days regardless of drift to ensure it is always current
Switch to a simpler rule-based model that does not require monitoring

questions.explanation: B is correct because the standard approach for handling ground truth latency is a two-layer monitoring strategy: (1) leading indicators that are available immediately — prediction score distributions, input feature drift, proxy business metrics (click-through rates, etc.) — which flag potential issues before labels arrive; and (2) lagged true quality metrics computed on the 14-day rolling window of labeled data. Together, these provide both early warning signals and ground-truth validation. A is too conservative — proxy monitoring is valuable during the label latency window. C is unconditional retraining that may be unnecessary if the model is stable. D abandons the ML system entirely, which is not a monitoring strategy.
questions.reference: Monitoring — two-layer strategy for ground truth latency: proxy indicators + lagged labels

questions.stem: A team observes that their classification model's predicted positive rate has increased from 5% at training time to 22% in production. The input feature distributions appear stable. What type of drift is this, and what is the most likely explanation?

options.text: Data drift — the input features have shifted even though the KS test shows no significant change

Prediction drift (output drift) — the model's predicted label distribution has shifted, potentially indicating the true positive rate in the population has genuinely increased, or concept drift has caused the model to misclassify
Model version drift — a different model version was accidentally deployed
Label drift — the training labels were imbalanced, causing the model to over-predict positives

questions.explanation: B is correct because when input feature distributions are stable but the predicted label distribution shifts (5% → 22% positive rate), this is prediction drift or output drift. The explanation can be either: (a) the true positive rate in the population has genuinely increased (e.g., more fraud, more churn), which would be appropriate model behavior; or (b) concept drift — the model's learned P(Y|X) is no longer calibrated to the true relationship, causing it to over-predict positives. Investigating with actual labels (once available) is needed to distinguish the two. A is wrong because data drift refers to P(X) change, which is ruled out by stable feature distributions. C (version drift) would typically cause sudden discontinuous changes, not gradual shifts. D (label drift) refers to changes in training labels, not production prediction distribution.
questions.reference: Monitoring — prediction drift with stable inputs: genuine population change vs concept drift

questions.stem: A team uses Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring on an inference table with a TIMESERIES monitor. They notice the daily accuracy metric in the drift metrics table is NULL for several days. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Accuracy cannot be computed for TIMESERIES monitors — use InferenceLog type instead
The ground truth label column contains NULL values for those days, making accuracy computation impossible
The monitoring job failed on those days and the metrics were not written
The inference table had fewer rows than the minimum threshold for those time windows, causing Lakehouse Monitoring to suppress metrics to avoid statistically unreliable results

questions.explanation: D is correct because Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring applies a minimum row count threshold per time window before computing aggregate statistics and model quality metrics. If a particular daily window contains too few rows (e.g., during a low-traffic weekend or a data pipeline outage that delayed writes), the monitor suppresses metrics for that window rather than reporting unreliable statistics computed from very small samples. A is wrong — TIMESERIES monitors do compute accuracy if labels are available. B is partially plausible but NULLs in labels would typically result in metric computation on non-null rows, not complete suppression. C is possible but would affect all metrics, not just accuracy for specific windows.
questions.reference: Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring — minimum row count threshold for metric computation

questions.stem: An ML platform team is designing monitoring for a recommendation system. They cannot directly observe ground truth (whether users would have clicked a recommendation they didn't see). Select TWO proxy monitoring approaches appropriate for this case.

options.text: Track the click-through rate (CTR) on recommended items as a business proxy metric for recommendation quality
Compute precision@k directly against future purchases as the ground truth, ignoring the causality problem
Monitor the distribution of recommendation score outputs for drift — a significant shift in score distribution without input drift may indicate model degradation
Replace the ML model with a rule-based system to eliminate the ground truth latency problem

questions.explanation: A is correct because CTR on recommended items is a real-time, observable business proxy metric that correlates with recommendation quality. A sustained decline in CTR (after controlling for seasonal effects) is an actionable signal of model degradation even without explicit relevance labels. C is correct because monitoring recommendation score distributions provides a statistical proxy — if the model starts assigning systematically different scores to similar item-user pairs (e.g., all scores near 0.5 indicating low confidence), this signals potential model issues without requiring ground truth labels. B is wrong because future purchase data suffers from severe selection bias (users can only buy what they were shown), making precision@k against purchases a biased metric. D abandons ML monitoring rather than solving it.
questions.reference: Monitoring — recommendation systems: proxy metrics (CTR, score distribution) for unobservable ground truth
